name: Initialize Go Module with Proper Structure
description: Set up a new Go project with module initialization, standard directory layout, and essential boilerplate files.
trendingReason: Go remains the top choice for cloud-native backends and CLI tools

steps.title: Create project directory and initialize module
steps.description: Create a new directory and initialize a Go module with your module path.
steps.command: mkdir myapp && cd myapp && go mod init github.com/yourname/myapp
steps.tip: Use your GitHub path as the module name for easy import by others.

steps.title: Create standard directory layout
steps.description: Set up the conventional Go project structure with cmd, internal, and pkg directories.
steps.tip: The internal/ directory prevents external packages from importing its contents.

steps.title: Create the main entry point
steps.description: Write a minimal main.go file in the cmd directory as the application entry point.
steps.command:
cat > cmd/myapp/main.go << 'EOF'
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Hello from myapp")
}
EOF

steps.title: Add a Makefile for common tasks
steps.description: Create a Makefile with build, test, and lint targets for a consistent developer workflow.
steps.command:
cat > Makefile << 'EOF'
.PHONY: build test lint run

build:
	go build -o bin/myapp ./cmd/myapp

test:
	go test ./... -v

lint:
	golangci-lint run ./...

run:
	go run ./cmd/myapp
EOF

steps.title: Build and run the project
steps.description: Verify the setup by building and running the application.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Go Project Setup
set -euo pipefail

PROJECT=${1:?"Usage: $0 <project-name>"}
MODULE=${2:-"github.com/yourname/$PROJECT"}

echo "=== Creating Go project: $PROJECT ==="
mkdir -p "$PROJECT"
cd "$PROJECT"

go mod init "$MODULE"

mkdir -p cmd/"$PROJECT" internal/config internal/handler pkg/utils

cat > cmd/"$PROJECT"/main.go << 'GOEOF'
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Hello from the project")
}
GOEOF

cat > Makefile << 'MKEOF'
.PHONY: build test lint run clean

build:
	go build -o bin/app ./cmd/$(notdir $(CURDIR))

test:
	go test ./... -v -race

lint:
	golangci-lint run ./...

run:
	go run ./cmd/$(notdir $(CURDIR))

clean:
	rm -rf bin/
MKEOF

echo ""
echo "=== Project created ==="
echo "Build:  make build"
echo "Test:   make test"
echo "Run:    make run"
echo "Lint:   make lint"

faq.question: What is the difference between internal/ and pkg/?
faq.answer: Code in internal/ cannot be imported by other modules outside your project. Code in pkg/ is explicitly meant to be importable by external consumers. Use internal/ by default.

faq.question: Should I vendor my dependencies?
faq.answer: Vendoring (go mod vendor) is useful for reproducible builds in CI or air-gapped environments. For most projects, the module cache is sufficient.

faq.question: What Go version should I target in go.mod?
faq.answer: Set the minimum Go version your project supports. Use 'go mod tidy -go=1.21' to set a specific version. Newer features will not be available if you target an older version.
